g.data-narration: Welcome to Pseudocolor Image Processing - a powerful technique for enhancing grayscale images using color.
Pseudocolor assigns colors to grayscale intensity values. Unlike true color, colors are arbitrarily chosen to enhance interpretation.
g.text: Technique that assigns colors to gray values based on a specific criterion.
Colors enhance details not naturally visible to the human eye.

g.data-narration: Human eyes see thousands of color shades but only ~24 shades of gray. Pseudocolor exploits color perception to reveal hidden details.
g.text: We distinguish thousands of color shades but only ~24 shades of gray.
Pseudocolor transforms subtle intensity variations into visible color differences.

g.data-narration: True color represents natural hues from RGB sensors. Pseudocolor arbitrarily maps intensity to color for data enhancement.
Intensity slicing divides the grayscale range (0-255) into intervals, assigning each interval a specific color like a contour map.
g.text: Dividing grayscale range into intervals, assigning each a specific color.
Think of it like a contour map where elevation ranges get different colors.

g.data-narration: Plane slicing treats intensity as height in 3D. Slicing with parallel planes creates colored contours showing intensity levels.
g.text: Treat grayscale as 3D surface where intensity = height.
Slicing with parallel planes creates colored contours.

g.data-narration: LUT method uses a 256-entry table mapping each gray level to an RGB color. Fast and efficient - single memory lookup per pixel.
g.text: 256-entry table mapping gray levels (0-255) to RGB colors.
Extremely fast - single memory access per pixel. Easy to swap color schemes.

g.data-narration: Advanced pseudocolor uses three transformation functions - one each for R, G, B channels based on input intensity.
g.text: Three independent transformation functions for R, G, B components.

g.data-narration: Example transformations: sinusoidal red, quadratic green, square root blue create smooth non-linear color mappings.
g.text: Different mappings create different color schemes for emphasizing intensity ranges.

g.data-narration: Three color schemes: Sequential for ordered data, Diverging for midpoint data, Qualitative for categorical data.
Two implementation methods: LUT for speed (table lookup), Transformation functions for dynamic/adaptive coloring.
g.text: 2. For each pixel: Read intensity I

g.data-narration: Key applications: Medical imaging (X-rays, MRI), Remote sensing (satellite, NDVI), Scientific visualization (temperature, stress).
Remember: Pseudocolor enhances grayscale by mapping intensity to color. Use LUT for speed, functions for flexibility. Choose colors based on data type.
g.text: • Pseudocolor assigns arbitrary colors to grayscale intensity values
• Intensity slicing divides range into intervals, each assigned a color
• LUT method: Fast table lookup. Transform functions: Flexible but slower
